request.question: What do both Norwegian Lundehund and Kerry Blue Terrier have in coman

title: Blue (Don Cherry's dog)
Norwegian Lundehund
Torums Scarf Michael
Chamuco
Storhamar Ishockey
Blue Paul Terrier
Kerry Blue Terrier
So Easy
Mariusgenser
Norwegian Confederation of Trade Unions
sentences: Blue was a white Bull Terrier owned by hockey commentator Don Cherry.
Blue was reportedly a gift from the members of the Boston Bruins when Cherry was their head coach from 1974 to 1979.
The original Blue, who died in 1989, was a female.
Cherry has since owned other white bull terriers, all of which were named Blue.
The Norwegian Lundehund ("Norsk Lundehund") is a small dog breed of the Spitz type that originates from Norway.
Its name is a compound noun composed of the elements "Lunde", meaning puffin (Norwegian "lunde" "puffin" or "lundefugl" "puffin bird"), and "hund", meaning dog.
The breed was originally developed for the hunting of puffins and their eggs.
Ch.

Torums Scarf Michael (aka Mick or Mike) (29 May 1996 – October 3, 2011) in Liverpool, England, is a Kerry Blue Terrier who is best known for being the 2000 Best in Show winner at Crufts, and 2003 Best in Show of the Westminster Kennel Club Dog Show.
He is the first dog to win the "Triple Crown" of dog shows, having also won the 2002 AKC/Eukanuba National Invitational Championship.
The Chamuco (a Mexican word which means "devil") or Mexican Pitbull is a dog breed not recognized by any Canofile association.

It originated in the center of Mexico, it was developed in this country in the 1970s, product of the accidental or intentional crosses of the American Pit Bull Terrier with the now extinct Mexican Bulldog, street dogs, American Bully, Pitbull Blue, Staffordshire bull terrier, American Staffordshire Terrier, and probably with the Boxer.
Its name comes from the Mexican slang "Chamuco", which means devil because of its temperament and tenacity.
Also it called Mexican pitbull or miniature pitbull, but actually is Dogo of Mexican origin.
Storhamar Hockey is a Norwegian ice hockey team based in Hamar, Norway.
The club currently plays in the GET-ligaen, the highest level of Norwegian hockey.
The club has won seven regular season titles and six national championships since its founding on 18 March 1957.
Storhamar play their home games in the CC Amfi.
The club also includes the largest junior department in Norwegian ice hockey.
The team colours are yellow and blue.
Blue Paul Terrier is an extinct breed of dog.
The Kerry Blue Terrier (also known as the Irish Blue Terrier) () is a breed of dog.

Originally bred to control "vermin" including rats, rabbits, badgers, foxes, otters and hares, over time the Kerry became a general working dog used for a variety of jobs including herding cattle and sheep, and as a guard dog.
Today the Kerry has spread around the world as a companion and working dog.

Despite a Kerry Blue winning Crufts (the most important UK dog show) in 2000, it remains an "unfashionable" breed, and is distinctly uncommon; however, it not as threatened as some of the other terrier breeds such as Skye Terrier, Sealyham Terrier, and Dandie Dinmont Terrier.
"So Easy" is a song by Norwegian duo Röyksopp, released as their first single.
It was first made available in 1999, with only 500 copies made and distributed.

It was later re-released on Röyksopp's debut album "Melody A.M." "So Easy" contains instrumental and vocal samples from a 1960s cover version of the Burt Bacharach/Hal David song "Blue on Blue" (often misheard as "Who are you") recorded by a Swedish vocal group called Gals & Pals.
The sampled lyrics are "Blue on blue, heartache on heartache/Blue on blue, Now that we are through."
Marius Sweaters (Norwegian: "Mariusgenser" ) are Norwegian-style knitted sweaters with patterns inspired by traditional Setesdal sweater (Norwegian: "Setesdalsgenser" ), but without lice.

While the traditional sweaters used the natural colors of sheep wool - black, gray, brown and white - Marius sweater designs incorporated colors, with the most common being red, white and blue, the colors of the Norwegian flag.

The Norwegian Confederation of Trade Unions (Norwegian: "Landsorganisasjonen i Norge" , LO) is a national trade union center, decidedly the largest and probably the most influential umbrella organization of labour unions in Norway.
The 21 national unions affiliated to the LO have almost 900,000 members of a Norwegian population of 5 million.

The majority of affiliated unions organizes traditional blue collar workers, but the largest affiliate is the Norwegian Union of Municipal and General Employees which makes up more than a third of all members.
LO is affiliated to the ITUC and the ETUC.
Gerd Kristiansen was elected leader on 6 May 2013 for a four-year period.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

hop1_docs: Kerry Blue Terrier | The Kerry Blue Terrier (also known as the Irish Blue Terrier) () is a breed of dog.  Originally bred to control "vermin" including rats, rabbits, badgers, foxes, otters and hares, over time the Kerry became a general working dog used for a variety of jobs including herding cattle and sheep, and as a guard dog.  Today the Kerry has spread around the world as a companion and working dog.  Despite a Kerry Blue winning Crufts (the most important UK dog show) in 2000, it remains an "unfashionable" breed, and is distinctly uncommon; however, it not as threatened as some of the other terrier breeds such as Skye Terrier, Sealyham Terrier, and Dandie Dinmont Terrier.

Norwegian Lundehund | The Norwegian Lundehund ("Norsk Lundehund") is a small dog breed of the Spitz type that originates from Norway.  Its name is a compound noun composed of the elements "Lunde", meaning puffin (Norwegian "lunde" "puffin" or "lundefugl" "puffin bird"), and "hund", meaning dog.  The breed was originally developed for the hunting of puffins and their eggs.

Fox Terrier | Fox Terriers are two different breeds of the terrier dog type: the Smooth Fox Terrier and the Wire Fox Terrier.  Both of these breeds originated in the 19th century from a handful of dogs who are descended from earlier varieties of British terriers, and are related to other modern white terrier breeds.  In addition, a number of breeds have diverged from these two main types of fox terrier and have been recognised separately, including the Jack Russell Terrier, Miniature Fox Terrier and Rat Terrier.  The Wire and Smooth Fox Terriers share similar characteristics, the main differences being in the coat and markings.  They have been successful in conformation shows, more prominently in America than their homeland.

Torums Scarf Michael (aka Mick or Mike) (29 May 1996 – October 3, 2011) in Liverpool, England, is a Kerry Blue Terrier who is best known for being the 2000 Best in Show winner at Crufts, and 2003 Best in Show of the Westminster Kennel Club Dog Show.  He is the first dog to win the "Triple Crown" of dog shows, having also won the 2002 AKC/Eukanuba National Invitational Championship.

What I Do | What I Do is the twelfth studio album by American country music artist Alan Jackson.  It was released on September 7, 2004, and produced four singles for Jackson on the Hot Country Songs charts: "Too Much of a Good Thing" and "Monday Morning Church" both reached #5, while "The Talkin' Song Repair Blues" and "USA Today" both reached #18.  Making it the first album of his career not to produce any #1 hits.

Primary topics: Kerry Blue Terrier; Norwegian Lundehund — both described as dog breeds.

- Kerry Blue Terrier: passage states it "'is a breed of dog'" and is "'also known as the Irish Blue Terrier'" and was "'Originally bred to control \"vermin\"'".
- Norwegian Lundehund: passage states it "'is a small dog breed of the Spitz type'" and "'originates from Norway'"; name variants include "'Norsk Lundehund'" and "'Lunde' meaning puffin' / 'hund' meaning dog'".
- Ambiguity/missing facts: none for the shared property — both passages explicitly call each a dog breed (no conflicting candidates).
- Next-hop retrieval (optional): "Dog" (article lede/infobox) to confirm the general definition of "breed of dog" — otherwise no additional retrieval needed.

response.summary_2: Both the Kerry Blue Terrier and the Norwegian Lundehund are breeds of dog. Kerry Blue Terrier was originally bred to control vermin and is also known as the Irish Blue Terrier. Norwegian Lundehund is a small Spitz-type breed from Norway that was developed for hunting puffins.
response.answer: They are both breeds of dog.
